report.executive_summary: Carbon Design System scores 62.5/100 (not ready) with 6 dimension-level blockers. The system has mature governance (82.1/100), strong component quality (81.2/100), and an exceptionally rich variable system (1804 variables, 4 theme modes). However, Figma-side documentation is critically absent: 0% component descriptions carry functional intent, 0.3% of variables have descriptions, and no motion tokens exist in the design file. The single most important action is porting intent descriptions from code-side MDX docs to Figma component set root entries.

0_prerequisites.narrative: Carbon is an explicitly multi-platform system (web + iOS + Android) with documented platform strategy and clear information architecture.
0_prerequisites.value_framing: Platform strategy is clear and multi-platform scope is well-documented, enabling agents to determine platform targets without guessing.

1_token_and_variable_system.narrative: Exceptionally rich variable system (1804 variables, 34 collections, 3175 aliases) with four theme modes. Three-layer architecture present in code. Figma variable descriptions nearly absent (0.3%).
1_token_and_variable_system.value_framing: Token architecture is rich in Figma (1804 variables, 4 themes) but near-zero descriptions mean an agent cannot determine token purpose from metadata alone, forcing inference from naming conventions and consuming excess tokens per interaction.

2_component_quality.narrative: Strong component architecture: 2856 published variants, comprehensive state coverage, TypeScript APIs, and meaningful token binding including spacing and typography on form components.
2_component_quality.value_framing: Strong component architecture in both Figma and code enables reliable agent composition, but inconsistent token binding depth means some components are more theme-aware than others.

3_documentation_and_intent.narrative: Severe split: code-side documentation is excellent (MDX, structured API docs, usage guidelines), but Figma-side descriptions are nearly absent (5.1% components, 0% sub-component doc frames). Co-location principle not satisfied: external docs exist but no declared path from Figma components.
3_documentation_and_intent.value_framing: Without functional descriptions in Figma, every AI-assisted component selection requires a human to intervene and re-specify. The excellent code-side documentation is invisible to agents consuming only the Figma API.

4_design_quality_baseline.narrative: Strong baseline quality across most dimensions. Comprehensive type scale, spacing system, colour structure, and accessibility patterns. Motion tokens exist in code but not in Figma Variables. Empty state patterns absent.
4_design_quality_baseline.value_framing: Strong baseline quality across most dimensions reduces agent correction cycles, but missing motion tokens in Figma and absent empty state patterns force case-by-case decisions.

5_governance_and_ecosystem.narrative: Mature governance: documented deprecation patterns with runtime warnings, comprehensive testing (unit + AVT + VRT), monorepo with clear contribution standards, and adoption tracking via @carbon/react npm.
5_governance_and_ecosystem.value_framing: Mature governance with formal deprecation patterns, comprehensive testing, and documented contribution standards reduces agent errors from outdated or inconsistent patterns.

6_design_to_code_parity.narrative: Structural parity is strong (component naming, variant coverage) but token-level parity is difficult to assess due to different architectures. 518 code-only tokens, 1164 Figma-only tokens, no parity gap register.
6_design_to_code_parity.value_framing: Undocumented parity gaps across 1682 tokens are silent error sources. Different token architectures between Figma and code are undocumented, blocking automated drift detection.

0.1_platform_architecture_clarity.narrative: Carbon is documented as a multi-platform system with web as primary and native guidelines published. The Figma file is web-focused but references cross-platform considerations. Platform strategy is discoverable from the docs site and file structure. An agent can determine platform targets without guessing.

1.1_token_implementation.narrative: 1804 variables across 34 collections covering colour, spacing, typography, breakpoints, grid, layer, and component-specific tokens. All implemented as Figma Variables with mode support. Four theme modes (White, Gray 10, Gray 90, Gray 100). 342 styles (218 fills, 116 effects, 8 grids). Token coverage is comprehensive across all design property categories.

1.2_alias_chain_integrity.narrative: 3175 alias references across 1804 variables (176% ratio, meaning most variables alias through multiple hops). Theme collection (548 vars) aliases to Colors primitives (135 vars). Component-specific collections alias to Theme. However, code-side component tokens in packages/themes/src/component-tokens use hardcoded hex values rather than aliasing semantic tokens, breaking the three-layer chain at the component level.

1.3_token_architecture_depth.narrative: Three layers confirmed in both Figma and code. Primitives: Colors collection (135 vars) + Type Primitives (329 vars). Semantic: Theme collection (548 vars) with four modes. Component: dedicated collections (Button, Content Switcher, etc.) and contextual variables. Code mirrors this with @carbon/colors (primitives), @carbon/themes (semantic), and component-tokens/ (component). Minor gap: code-side component tokens hardcode hex values instead of aliasing semantic tokens.

1.4_primitive_naming.narrative: Full words throughout. Color primitives use hue+step convention (blue60, gray10). Spacing uses numbered scale (spacing-01 through spacing-13). Typography uses role-based names (body-compact-01, heading-03). Consistent within categories. The hue+step pattern for primitives is functional but not fully intent-based.

1.5_token_format_machine_readability.narrative: Code-side tokens are JS module exports with computed values (rem(), miniUnits(), adjustAlpha()). SCSS generated via @carbon/scss-generator. No DTCG or Style Dictionary interop. Format is machine-readable but requires Carbon-specific tooling to parse. An AI agent needs custom extraction logic rather than standard token tooling.

1.6_token_documentation.narrative: 5 of 1804 variables (0.3%) have descriptions in Figma. This is worse than MUI (18.4%). No variable descriptions on the Colors primitives (135 vars), Theme semantics (548 vars), spacing, typography, or breakpoints collections. Without descriptions, an agent cannot determine token purpose from the variable data alone.

2.1_component_to_token_binding.narrative: MCP spot-check of Tag (Purple, Small, Enabled) shows color fills bound to variables (tag-background, tag-color, tag-border-operational) and typography via label-01 style. Accordion shows text bound to text-primary, border to border-subtle-background, typography via body-01 style with full font specs. Button triggered Code Connect prompt suggesting minimal native bindings. Token binding is present but inconsistent across components: form components (Text Input, Accordion) show richer binding than atomic components (Button, Tag).

2.2_component_api_composability.narrative: Full TypeScript with exported interfaces. ButtonBaseProps extends React.ButtonHTMLAttributes. Polymorphic 'as' prop via PolymorphicComponentPropWithRef. Constrained enums (ButtonKinds, ButtonSizes as const arrays with derived union types). Composable renderIcon prop accepts React.ElementType. Dual PropTypes + TypeScript typing. 126 components under @carbon/react.

2.3_variant_completeness.narrative: 2856 published components with exhaustive variant coverage. Button alone has 420 variants across Style (Primary, Secondary, Tertiary, Danger, Ghost, Danger ghost), Type (Text, Text + Icon, Icon only), Size (Small, Medium, Large, Extra large, 2X large, Expressive), and State (Enabled, Hover, Focus, Active, Disabled, Skeleton). Interactive states consistently defined. 4047 total variants across 196 component sets.

2.4_escape_hatch_usage.narrative: Carbon's API design constrains customisation through typed props and constrained enums. className is accepted on all components (standard React pattern). No sx prop or style prop abuse detected in examples. The design system's coding style guide in developer-handbook.md establishes BEM naming conventions that discourage direct style overrides.

3.1_component_description_coverage.narrative: 147 of 2856 components (5.1%) have any description in Figma. All 147 are spec implementation notes on Tree View variants ('Follow specs for levels...'), not functional intent. No component has an intent-carrying description. The code-side MDX documentation is excellent but is not accessible through the Figma API. Per the v3.0 co-location principle, external documentation with no declared path from the component scores lower — Carbon has no link fields or CLAUDE.md declarations bridging Figma components to code docs.

3.2_documentation_structure_machine_read.narrative: Code-side documentation uses MDX (Storybook-integrated) with doctoc-generated tables of contents. No frontmatter metadata. No structured JSON schema for component docs. Content is prose-based, not agent-optimised. API documentation is structured via Storybook ArgsTable. External docs on carbondesignsystem.com are HTML, not machine-readable data.

3.3_intent_quality.narrative: Code-side MDX covers 4 of 6 documentation hierarchy levels: Purpose (overview paragraphs with intent), Structure (via API docs), Intended behaviour (variant guidance), and Main use cases (when to use danger vs tertiary). Missing: Error handling and Edge cases. Figma-side has zero intent documentation. The split between excellent code docs and absent Figma docs creates a parity problem for agents consuming both sources.

3.4_usage_guidance_formalisation.narrative: Formalised usage guidance on carbondesignsystem.com with dedicated usage, style, code, and accessibility pages per component. Storybook provides interactive examples with Stackblitz integration. MDX files link to external usage pages. Guidance is structured, not ad-hoc. Per the co-location principle, code-side guidance is well-declared and accessible from the code repo; the Figma-side gap is scored under 3.1 and 3.5.

3.5_documentation_frame_metadata.narrative: Generic parser found 196 sub-components across 53 pages with rich structural data (4047 variants, 447 variant axes). However, 0 of 196 sub-components have descriptions in the Figma frame structure. Page-level descriptions exist (51 of 53 pages) but these are extracted from page content, not structured documentation frames. Carbon does not use a documentation frame convention. The Slot component has a description (tutorial link), but this is an exception, not a pattern.

4.1_interaction_targets.narrative: Carbon specifies minimum touch targets per component size variant. Button sizes range from Small (32px height) to 2XL (80px). Interactive targets meet WCAG 2.5.5 (44x44px) at Medium and above. Small variants are documented as desktop-only. Explicit size variants make target sizes inspectable.

4.2_contrast_ratios.narrative: Carbon's four-theme system (White, Gray 10, Gray 90, Gray 100) is designed around WCAG AA contrast compliance. Semantic token aliases (text-primary, text-secondary, text-on-color) resolve differently per theme to maintain contrast. Token value chain is auditable: text-primary resolves to gray100 (#161616) on white theme, white (#ffffff) on gray100 theme.

4.3_type_scale_consistency.narrative: 23-step type scale with mathematical progression: 12, 14, 16, 18, 20, 24, 28, 32, 36, 42, 48, 54, 60, 68, 76, 84, 92, 102, 112, 122, 132, 144, 156. Steps follow a consistent ratio. 329 Type Primitives in Figma. Scale is explicitly documented in code (packages/type/src/scale.js).

4.4_type_completeness.narrative: Code-side type styles define fontSize, fontWeight, lineHeight, and letterSpacing per role. Font family is IBM Plex Sans. MCP spot-check confirms typography bindings include weight, family, and paragraph spacing on Accordion (body-01: size 14, weight 400, lineHeight 20, letterSpacing 0.16) and Tag (label-01: size 12, lineHeight 16, letterSpacing 0.32). Line height and letter spacing are present but Figma variable binding for these properties is inconsistent across components.

4.5_spacing_scale_regularity.narrative: Carbon spacing scale uses 8px base unit (miniUnits). Scale: 2, 4, 8, 12, 16, 24, 32, 40, 48, 64, 80, 96, 160px. Implemented as both Figma Variables (28 spacing vars in local collection) and code tokens (spacing01-spacing13). Consistent progression with no irregular gaps.

4.6_grid_and_layout_system.narrative: Comprehensive grid system with 5 breakpoints (SM 320px, MD 672px, LG 1056px, XL 1312px, Max 1584px), column counts (4, 8, 16), and four grid modes (Wide, Narrow, Condensed, Nested). Grid mode collection has 24 variables. Breakpoint collection has 212 variables with mode-specific values. 8 grid styles published.

4.7_motion_duration_ranges.narrative: Motion tokens are well-defined in code (fast01: 70ms, fast02: 110ms, moderate01: 150ms, moderate02: 240ms, slow01: 400ms, slow02: 700ms) but do not exist as Figma Variables. No motion-related variable collection was found in the Figma file. An agent reading only the Figma file would find no motion specifications.

4.8_motion_easing.narrative: Code defines structured easing curves: standard, entrance, and exit categories, each with productive and expressive variants (6 easing curves total). All use cubic-bezier, no linear. However, these exist only in code (packages/motion/src/index.ts), not in Figma Variables or styles. Partial score because the system is well-designed but not available in the design tool.

4.9_focus_state_presence.narrative: Focus state present on all interactive components. Button has explicit Focus variant. Accordion has Focus state. Tag has interaction states. Carbon's accessibility-first approach includes AVT (Accessibility Verification Testing) with keyboard navigation tests tagged @avt-keyboard-nav on 82 components.

4.10_error_state_coverage.narrative: Error states present on form components. Text Input has Error variant with support-error token binding. Notification component has Status variants (Error, Warning, Success, Info). Form component includes error state patterns. support-error (#da1e28) and text-error tokens are bound to error-state components.

4.11_loading_state_coverage.narrative: Dedicated Loading component with 4 sub-components. Button has Skeleton state variant. However, not all async-capable components have loading patterns defined. Skeleton states exist but are not universal across the component library.

4.12_empty_state_coverage.narrative: No empty state patterns found in the Figma component library. Data Table, Dropdown, and other data-dependent components do not include empty state variants. No empty state utility component exists.

4.13_colour_system_structure.narrative: Structured colour system with 135 primitive colours (Colors collection), 548 semantic theme tokens with four modes, and contextual layer variables. Role-based naming (text-primary, background, layer-01, support-error). 10-step scales per hue. 218 fill styles published. The four-theme architecture is one of the most mature observed.

4.14_icon_sizing_consistency.narrative: Carbon defines icon sizes aligned with spacing scale. Code exports iconSize01 and iconSize02. Button Icon variants use consistent sizing across size variants. The icon-on-color token is bound to icon components. MCP spot-check on Accordion confirms chevron icon at 16x16px.

4.15_elevation_shadow_system.narrative: 116 effect styles published in Figma covering shadows and overlays. Carbon's elevation approach uses Layer tokens (Background, Layer 01, Layer 02) rather than shadow-based elevation like Material Design. This is a deliberate architectural choice. Shadows exist for specific components (Popover, Tooltip, Modal) rather than as a universal scale.

4.16_visibility_of_system_status.narrative: Systematically addressed. Notification component with 4 status variants. Loading component. Progress bar and Progress indicator components. Inline notification and Toast notification patterns.

4.17_match_between_system_and_real_world.narrative: Systematically addressed. Consistent, plain language naming (Button, Accordion, Breadcrumb, Toggle). No jargon in component names. Documentation on carbondesignsystem.com uses plain language with real-world task framing.

4.18_user_control_and_freedom.narrative: Systematically addressed. Modal has dismiss/close patterns. Notification has dismissible variants. Form components support clear/reset. Breadcrumb provides navigation context.

4.19_consistency_and_standards.narrative: Systematically addressed. Consistent naming conventions (PascalCase components, kebab-case tokens). Consistent size variants across components (Small, Medium, Large, Extra large). Consistent state naming (Enabled, Hover, Focus, Active, Disabled).

4.20_error_prevention.narrative: Systematically addressed. Form validation patterns. Constrained prop types prevent invalid configurations. Disabled states prevent interaction with unavailable actions. TypeScript catches prop errors at compile time.

4.21_recognition_rather_than_recall.narrative: Systematically addressed. Variant names describe their purpose (Style=Primary, State=Enabled). Component names are standard industry terms. Storybook provides visual documentation of all variants.

4.22_flexibility_and_efficiency.narrative: Partially addressed. Polymorphic 'as' prop on Button. renderIcon prop for icon composition. However, Carbon's API is intentionally constrained to enforce consistency, which limits flexibility for power users. No sx-style escape hatch.

4.23_aesthetic_and_minimalist_design.narrative: Systematically addressed. IBM's design philosophy emphasises minimal, functional design. Component variants are purposeful, not decorative. The layer-based elevation system avoids shadow overuse.

4.24_error_recovery.narrative: Partially addressed. Notification component includes error and warning variants. Form error states visible. However, no dedicated error recovery patterns (retry, undo) found as reusable components. Error recovery guidance exists on the docs site but not as Figma component patterns.

4.25_help_and_documentation.narrative: Partially addressed. Tooltip and Toggletip components provide contextual help. External documentation is comprehensive. However, no in-component help patterns (inline help text, guided tours) exist as reusable components. Help is available externally but not embedded in the system.

4.26_visual_hierarchy.narrative: Partially addressed. Type scale establishes heading hierarchy. Layer system (Background, Layer 01, Layer 02) creates depth. However, the hierarchy depends on correct token usage which is not enforced by the system. No visual hierarchy guidelines found as structured component-level documentation in Figma.

4.27_visual_rhythm_and_proportion.narrative: Partially addressed. 8px base grid with consistent spacing scale. Grid system with defined column and margin ratios. However, rhythm and proportion are emergent from token usage rather than explicitly documented as design principles in the Figma file.

5.1_naming_convention_consistency.narrative: Consistent PascalCase for components in code (Button, Accordion, DataTable). Figma page names match component names. Token names use kebab-case (spacing-01, body-compact-01). Minor inconsistency: some Figma variable collections use spaces (Color palette 1) while code uses camelCase (spacing01). The naming is consistent within each domain but conventions differ between Figma and code.

5.2_versioning_and_changelog_discipline.narrative: Monorepo managed with Lerna + Yarn workspaces. Individual packages versioned (v11.50+). Migration documentation at docs/migration/v11.md. @carbon/upgrade codemod package for automated migration. Release schedule documented. Per-package changelogs may exist but no single authoritative CHANGELOG at repo root.

5.3_contribution_standards.narrative: Comprehensive developer handbook at docs/developer-handbook.md covering commit conventions, coding style, BEM class naming, Sass documentation, file/folder conventions, deprecation, and publishing. Additional docs for experimental code, preview code, feature flags, and testing. No root CONTRIBUTING.md (unconventional location).

5.4_deprecation_patterns.narrative: Formal deprecation pattern using warning() utility. Runtime deprecation warnings in development mode. Components continue to work through current major version. Explicit notice of removal in next major. Bug fixes deprioritised but external contributions accepted. This is one of the best deprecation strategies observed in a design system.

5.5_test_coverage.narrative: Three-tier testing: (1) Unit tests with Jest + @testing-library/react covering rendering, props, states, keyboard interaction. (2) Accessibility Verification Testing (AVT) with Playwright, custom toHaveNoACViolations matcher, 82 of 126 components (65%) have e2e test directories with tagged test categories. (3) Visual regression via Playwright snapshots against Storybook. AVT tags flow to the docs site as component status indicators.

5.6_adoption_visibility.narrative: @carbon/react is published on npm with download tracking. The monorepo structure allows per-package version tracking. carbondesignsystem.com publishes component AVT statuses. No explicit dependency consumption dashboard or adoption metrics tool found, but npm statistics provide baseline visibility.

5.7_code_consistency_and_pattern_predict.narrative: 126 components follow consistent patterns: TypeScript interfaces, PropTypes, co-located MDX docs, test directories. Coding style guide establishes BEM naming, Sass conventions, and file organisation. Feature flags and experimental/preview patterns are formally documented. Minor inconsistency: some components have complete test coverage, others lack e2e tests (35% gap).

6.1_token_value_parity.narrative: Token diff found 116 matched tokens (via colour value matching), 6 value mismatches, 518 code-only tokens, and 1164 Figma-only tokens. The 95% match rate among comparable tokens is high, but the total matchable set is small (122 of 1924 total tokens = 6.3%). The large gap reflects different token architectures rather than value drift.

6.2_token_naming_parity.narrative: 0 naming mismatches among matched tokens (100% naming parity in the matched set). However, matching relied on colour value matching rather than name matching, indicating that naming conventions differ significantly between Figma and code. Figma uses slash-separated paths (Theme/text-primary), code uses camelCase (textPrimary) or kebab-case (text-primary). The naming gap is architectural, not accidental.

6.3_component_naming_parity.narrative: Component names align well between Figma and code. Figma page names (Button, Accordion, Data table, Text input) match code component names (Button, Accordion, DataTable, TextInput) after case normalisation. 47 Figma pages with components map to the 126 code components.

6.4_variant_and_state_coverage_parity.narrative: Figma has 2856 published component variants, code has 126 top-level components with variant props. State coverage is consistent: Enabled, Hover, Focus, Active, Disabled in both. Button size variants match between Figma and code. Minor gaps: code may support additional programmatic states (loading, skeleton) not visible as Figma variants on all components.

6.5_behaviour_parity.narrative: Figma captures static states but not transitions or interactive behaviour. Code implements keyboard navigation, focus management, aria attributes, and event handlers that have no Figma representation. The MDX documentation bridges some gaps with behavioural descriptions, but an agent reading only the Figma file cannot infer interaction patterns.

6.6_documentation_of_parity_gaps.narrative: No parity gap register exists. The token diff reveals 518 code-only tokens and 1164 Figma-only tokens with no documentation explaining which gaps are intentional (architectural differences) vs which are bugs. The naming convention differences (slash-paths vs camelCase) are undocumented. Motion tokens existing only in code is undocumented. Without a gap register, an agent cannot distinguish intentional omissions from drift.

AC-001.summary: Code-side component tokens hardcode hex values instead of aliasing semantic tokens
AC-001.description: Component tokens in packages/themes/src/component-tokens/ (button, notification, tag, status, content-switcher) define per-theme values with hardcoded hex fallbacks rather than aliasing the semantic theme tokens. This breaks the three-layer alias chain at the component level: an agent following aliases from component tokens will hit a dead end rather than resolving through semantic to primitive.
AC-001.recommendation: Refactor code-side component tokens to alias the semantic theme tokens (e.g., button-primary should alias interactive-01 or background-brand) rather than duplicating hex values per theme.

TF-001.summary: Custom JS token format requires Carbon-specific extraction tooling
TF-001.description: Carbon tokens are JS module exports with computed values (rem(), miniUnits(), adjustAlpha()). The @carbon/scss-generator builds SCSS output. No DTCG (Design Token Community Group) or Style Dictionary format support. An AI agent or external tooling needs custom extraction logic to read Carbon tokens.
TF-001.recommendation: Consider publishing tokens in DTCG format alongside the JS exports. This would enable standard tooling interop without replacing the existing JS-primary workflow.

TD-001.summary: 0.3% of Figma variables have descriptions — agent cannot determine token purpose
TD-001.description: Only 5 of 1804 Figma variables (0.3%) have descriptions. None of the major collections have descriptions: Colors primitives (135), Theme semantics (548), Spacing (28), Type Primitives (329), Breakpoint (212). Without descriptions, an agent reading the variable data cannot determine token purpose, scope, or constraints from the Figma API alone.
TD-001.recommendation: Add descriptions to all variable collections, prioritising Theme semantics (548 vars — these are the most-consumed tokens) and Colors primitives (135 vars — agents need to understand the scale). At minimum, add collection-level descriptions and descriptions for the 20 most-used semantic tokens.

CTB-001.summary: Token binding depth varies significantly across components
CTB-001.description: MCP spot-check reveals inconsistent token binding depth. Tag (Purple, Small, Enabled) has 3 variable bindings (tag-background, tag-color, tag-border-operational) plus typography. Accordion has richer bindings (text-primary, border-subtle, body-01 typography). Button triggered Code Connect prompt, suggesting minimal native bindings. The richer binding on form components demonstrates the system's capability, but simpler components are under-bound.
CTB-001.recommendation: Audit all components for token binding completeness. Use Accordion/Text Input binding depth as the target standard. Priority: ensure all interactive components have colour, typography, and spacing tokens bound.

CDC-001.summary: 94.9% of components have no Figma description — zero functional intent
CDC-001.description: 147 of 2856 components (5.1%) have any description in Figma. All 147 are Tree View spec notes ('Follow specs for levels...'), not functional intent descriptions. No component carries a description that would help an agent decide whether to use it. The code-side MDX documentation is excellent but is not accessible through the Figma API. Per the v3.0 co-location principle, external documentation with no declared path from the component is undeclared — Carbon has no link fields or CLAUDE.md declarations bridging Figma components to code docs.
CDC-001.recommendation: Add functional intent descriptions to the COMPONENT_SET root entries for the 45 primary components (one per page). Start with the 10 most-used components. Use the code-side MDX overview paragraphs as the source — they already contain good intent descriptions that need to be ported to Figma.

DS-001.summary: MDX docs lack structured metadata for agent consumption
DS-001.description: Code-side documentation uses MDX with Storybook integration. Content is well-structured as prose but lacks machine-readable metadata: no frontmatter, no structured JSON schema, no semantic markup beyond headings. An agent can read the text but cannot extract structured fields (purpose, structure, behaviour, use cases) without NLP parsing.
DS-001.recommendation: Add YAML frontmatter to MDX files with structured fields: component_name, purpose (one sentence), category, status, related_components. This would make documentation machine-readable without changing the human-readable content.

IQ-001.summary: Code-side docs cover 4 of 6 hierarchy levels; Figma has zero intent
IQ-001.description: Against the six-level documentation hierarchy: code-side MDX covers Purpose (overview paragraphs), Structure (via API docs), Intended behaviour (variant guidance), and Main use cases (when-to-use guidance). Missing: Error handling and Edge cases. Figma-side has zero intent documentation. The split creates a problem: an agent reading Figma cannot access any intent, an agent reading code gets 4/6 levels.
IQ-001.recommendation: Priority 1: port one-line intent summaries from MDX to Figma component descriptions (fixes CDC-001 simultaneously). Priority 2: add Error handling and Edge cases sections to the 10 most-used component MDX files.

DFM-001.summary: Zero sub-component descriptions in Figma frame structure
DFM-001.description: 196 COMPONENT_SET nodes found across 53 pages with rich structural data (4047 variants, 447 variant axes). However, 0 of 196 have Figma descriptions on the COMPONENT_SET node. Carbon does not use a structured documentation frame convention. Page-level text exists (51 of 53 pages) but is embedded in the canvas, not in structured node metadata. The Slot component has a description (tutorial link) but this is an exception, not a pattern.
DFM-001.recommendation: Add descriptions to COMPONENT_SET nodes. These are the primary metadata entry points for agents using the Figma API. The description field is structured (not canvas text) and returned by the REST API components endpoint.

TC-001.summary: Typography variable binding in Figma is inconsistent across properties
TC-001.description: Code-side type styles define fontSize, fontWeight, lineHeight, and letterSpacing per role. MCP spot-check confirms Accordion binds body-01 (size 14, weight 400, lineHeight 20, letterSpacing 0.16) and Tag binds label-01 (size 12, lineHeight 16, letterSpacing 0.32) as composite styles. However, whether these bindings use individual variables or composite text styles is inconsistent across components.
TC-001.recommendation: Standardise typography binding approach: either bind all properties as individual variables or use composite text styles consistently. Document which approach is canonical.

MOT-001.summary: Motion duration tokens exist in code but not in Figma Variables
MOT-001.description: Code defines well-structured motion durations (fast01: 70ms, fast02: 110ms, moderate01: 150ms, moderate02: 240ms, slow01: 400ms, slow02: 700ms) with productive/expressive variants. These do not exist as Figma Variables or styles. An agent reading only the Figma file would find no motion specifications.
MOT-001.recommendation: Create a Motion variable collection in Figma with duration and easing tokens matching the code definitions. Alternatively, document the absence as intentional (Figma cannot animate) and ensure the motion specifications are referenced in component documentation frames.

MOT-002.summary: Easing curves well-designed in code but absent from Figma
MOT-002.description: Six easing curves defined in code (standard/entrance/exit x productive/expressive) using cubic-bezier. All curves use proper easing, no linear. However, these exist only in code. Figma does not support animation curves as Variables, but the values could be documented as text metadata or stored as named Figma styles.
MOT-002.recommendation: Document motion easing values in Figma either as variable descriptions or as a dedicated documentation page. Ensure motion specifications are discoverable from the Figma file.

LS-001.summary: Loading/skeleton states not universal across the component library
LS-001.description: Dedicated Loading component exists with 4 sub-components. Button has Skeleton state variant. However, not all async-capable components (Dropdown, Data Table, Select) have loading or skeleton patterns defined as Figma variants.
LS-001.recommendation: Add skeleton state variants to async-capable components (Data Table, Dropdown, Select) or document that the Loading component should be used as a wrapper pattern for these cases.

ES-001.summary: No empty state patterns in Figma component library
ES-001.description: Data-dependent components (Data Table, Dropdown, List, Contained list) do not include empty state variants. No dedicated empty state utility component exists. Empty states are a critical UX pattern for data-driven interfaces. Their absence forces designers and agents to improvise.
ES-001.recommendation: Create empty state variants for data-dependent components or create a reusable EmptyState component pattern. Document when to use empty states and what content they should contain.

TVP-001.summary: Only 6.3% of total tokens matched across platforms — different architectures
TVP-001.description: Token diff found 116 matched tokens with 95% value parity among matched set. However, total matchable set is small (122 of 1924 total = 6.3%). 518 code-only tokens and 1164 Figma-only tokens remain unmatched. The large gap reflects different token architectures: Figma uses mode-based contextual variables while code uses flat JS exports.
TVP-001.recommendation: The low match rate is architectural, not a quality issue. Document the architectural differences between Figma's mode-based variables and code's JS exports. Create a mapping document that explains how each Figma collection maps to code packages.

TVP-002.summary: Token naming differs architecturally between Figma and code
TVP-002.description: Matching relied on colour value comparison rather than name matching, indicating that naming conventions differ significantly. Figma: Theme/text-primary. Code: textPrimary. Figma: Colors/blue/60. Code: blue60. The gap is systematic and consistent — a deterministic transform could bridge it.
TVP-002.recommendation: Publish a naming convention mapping (Figma name <-> code export name) as a structured data file. This enables automated parity checking and reduces false positives in drift detection.

DPG-001.summary: No parity gap register — 1682 unmatched tokens undocumented
DPG-001.description: 518 code-only tokens and 1164 Figma-only tokens have no documentation explaining whether gaps are intentional or bugs. The naming convention differences are undocumented. Motion tokens existing only in code is undocumented. Without a gap register, automated drift detection will generate false positives, and agents cannot distinguish intentional omissions from actual gaps.
DPG-001.recommendation: Create a parity gap register documenting: (1) known architectural differences (mode-based variables vs flat exports), (2) intentional omissions (motion in code only, grid styles in Figma only), (3) naming convention mapping. Store as a structured JSON file alongside the token definitions.

REM-004.action: Document motion tokens in Figma. Either create a Motion variable collection with duration and easing tokens matching the code definitions, or create a documentation page in the Figma file that declares the code-side motion token values and links to packages/motion.
REM-004.value_framing: Motion specifications exist in code but are invisible from Figma. An agent building from Figma alone will produce static components with no motion guidance, requiring post-generation correction on every animated interaction.

REM-001.action: Add functional intent descriptions to Figma COMPONENT_SET root entries for all 45 primary components. Source text from existing code-side MDX overview paragraphs. Start with the 10 most-used components (Button, Data Table, Dropdown, Text Input, Accordion, Modal, Notification, Tabs, Checkbox, Toggle).
REM-001.value_framing: Without component descriptions, an AI agent cannot determine component purpose from the Figma API. Every agent interaction requires manual lookup, adding correction cycles to every design-to-code handoff.

REM-002.action: Add descriptions to Figma variable definitions, prioritising Theme semantics (548 vars) and Colors primitives (135 vars). At minimum, add collection-level descriptions and descriptions for the 20 most-consumed semantic tokens (text-primary, background, layer-01, support-error, interactive, border-strong, etc.).
REM-002.value_framing: Without token descriptions, an AI agent cannot distinguish tokens by purpose. Token selection errors propagate to every component generated, requiring theme rework on each affected component.

REM-003.action: Create a parity gap register as a structured JSON file documenting: (1) known architectural differences between Figma mode-based variables and code flat JS exports, (2) intentional omissions (motion in code only, grid styles in Figma only), (3) naming convention mapping (Figma slash-paths ↔ code camelCase/kebab-case).
REM-003.value_framing: Without a gap register, automated drift detection generates false positives on 1682 tokens. Every parity check requires manual triage, blocking CI/CD integration of token sync tooling.

REM-005.action: Add YAML frontmatter to MDX documentation files with structured fields: component_name, purpose, category, status, related_components. Apply to all 126 component MDX files. Auto-fixable with a script.
REM-005.value_framing: Without structured metadata, an agent must parse prose to extract component purpose and status. Frontmatter enables instant structured lookup, reducing per-component context processing.

REM-006.action: Publish naming convention mapping between Figma and code as a structured data file (JSON). Map Figma slash-separated paths to code camelCase/kebab-case exports. Enables automated parity checking.
REM-006.value_framing: Without a naming map, every token parity check must fall back to value matching. Name-based matching is faster, more reliable, and catches value drift that value-only matching misses.

REM-010.action: Add Error handling and Edge cases sections to the 10 most-used component MDX files (Button, Data Table, Dropdown, Text Input, Accordion, Modal, Notification, Form, Select, Tabs). This completes levels 5 and 6 of the documentation hierarchy.
REM-010.value_framing: Missing error handling and edge case documentation means agents produce components that work for happy paths but fail on boundary conditions. Each edge case failure requires a correction cycle.

REM-007.action: Consider publishing tokens in DTCG (Design Token Community Group) format alongside the existing JS exports. This enables standard tooling interop (Style Dictionary, token transformers) without replacing the JS-primary workflow.
REM-007.value_framing: Custom JS token format requires Carbon-specific extraction logic. DTCG format would enable any standard token tool to consume Carbon tokens without custom parsing, reducing integration effort for downstream teams.

REM-008.action: Refactor code-side component tokens (packages/themes/src/component-tokens/) to alias semantic theme tokens instead of hardcoding hex values. This completes the three-layer alias chain in code, matching the Figma architecture.
REM-008.value_framing: Broken alias chain at the component level means an agent following token references from component to primitive hits a dead end in code. Theme changes require manual hex updates in component-tokens/ instead of cascading automatically.

REM-009.action: Audit all components for token binding completeness. Use Text Input/Accordion binding depth as the target standard (colour + typography + spacing + contextual layer). Prioritise the 10 most-used interactive components.
REM-009.value_framing: Inconsistent token binding means an agent generates theme-aware code for some components but hardcoded values for others. This creates per-component rework when switching themes.

REM-011.action: Create empty state variants for data-dependent components (Data Table, Dropdown, List, Contained List) or create a reusable EmptyState component pattern with documentation on when and how to use it.
REM-011.value_framing: Without empty state patterns, every data-dependent view requires custom empty state design. This is a design quality gap that affects end-user experience and adds design debt to every consuming team.

REM-012.action: Add skeleton/loading state variants to async-capable components (Data Table, Dropdown, Select) or document that the Loading component should be used as a wrapper pattern for these cases.
REM-012.value_framing: Inconsistent loading patterns mean agents must decide per-component whether to use a dedicated skeleton or a wrapper Loading component. Documented patterns eliminate this ambiguity.

REM-013.action: Standardise typography binding in Figma: choose either individual variable bindings or composite text styles as the canonical approach, and apply consistently across all components. Document the chosen approach.
REM-013.value_framing: Inconsistent typography binding means an agent extracting type styles gets different data shapes from different components. Standardisation enables reliable automated typography extraction.
